FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 21 research sites, baseline cohort age 9-10, NDA collection #2147 (DOI: 10.15154/8873-zj65), 5.1 data release explicitly cited in Methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal mediation analysis of ABCD Years 2, 3, and 4 data using causal mediation framework (lme4 and mediation R packages), N=2,749.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 14(2025)3, 1368-1379; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. Exposures are gaming duration (self-reported hours/day of video gaming, platform unspecified) and problematic gaming measured by VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale). Per binding clarification #2, VGAQ and generic gaming duration without explicit mobile/phone modality do not qualify. No social media or smartphone variable appears in any main table, sensitivity, or appendix. Step 2 not reached. Gaming-only study with no qualifying phone/SM variable.

notes: Study examines caffeine use as mediator of gaming duration/VGAQ -> sleep outcomes. All variables are gaming, caffeine, and sleep; no smartphone or social media variable present anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development), 21 U.S. sites, NIH-funded, 11,868 participants baseline age 9-10, NDA DOI: 10.82525/jy7n-g441, release 6.0 cited in Methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: multilevel linear regression (Years 2-4) plus parallel mediation model (Mplus 8.10) examining ADHD -> gaming addiction pathways, N~7,260-9,604.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Psychiatry, 17:1756782, published 06 March 2026; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. The exposure is ADHD diagnosis (KSADS-COMP); the primary outcome is VGAQ score (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire). Per binding clarification #2, VGAQ is a gaming variable and does not qualify as a phone/SM variable. Mediators are psychosocial variables (family conflict, school involvement, prosocial peer involvement, parental monitoring, prosocial behavior, school disengagement, school environment, rule breaking, parent behavior inventory) - none are phone/SM. No smartphone or social media variable appears in any main table, supplementary table, or sensitivity analysis. Step 2 not reached. Gaming-only study with no qualifying phone/SM variable.

notes: Study examines ADHD -> gaming addiction (VGAQ) relationship with psychosocial mediators (prosocial peers, school involvement, family conflict). No smartphone or social media variable present anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study (Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development), 11,880 participants baseline age 9-10 across 21 research sites, ABCD Data Release 5.1 (January 2022) explicitly cited in Methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) of screen time and social media patterns plus multivariable linear regression of anxiety/depression outcomes, N=9,474, data from 2016-2022.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Public Health (2026) 26:262; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media use is measured and analyzed as a separate secondary exposure variable (hours per weekday/weekend, standardized across follow-ups), with its own GBTM trajectory groups and its own regression coefficients in Table 4, paired with anxiety and depression (individual-trait mental health outcomes) on the other side. Representative estimates: Group 2 (Gradual Increase to Moderate Use) vs Group 1: beta = -0.30 (95% CI: -0.58, -0.03, p=0.031) for anxiety; Groups 3, 4, 5 vs Group 1: significant betas for depression (e.g., Group 3 beta=1.03, 95% CI: 0.52, 1.55, p<0.001). Social media time is a qualifying phone/SM variable; anxiety/depression are qualifying individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression beta coefficients with 95% CIs reported in Table 3 (screen time trajectories vs anxiety/depression) and Table 4 (social media trajectories vs anxiety/depression), all paired with individual-trait mental health outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results present: beta coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values reported throughout Tables 3 and 4 for social media -> anxiety/depression relationships.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply: social media is the IV (exposure/predictor) in the analyses, not the DV. Anxiety and depression are the DVs. Because SM is used as IV, condition (b) of FT-EC7 (SM as DV in every analysis) is not met.
notes: Screen time (general, multi-device aggregate) also analyzed but social media is separately broken out with its own coefficients against anxiety/depression, providing the qualifying Step-1 hit for FT-IC2. INCLUDE.
